vocabulary.definition: Occurring, awarded, or appearing after the death of the originator. From Latin posthumus, from post- 'after' + humus 'ground, earth'.
vocabulary.context: George Carlin: I'm Glad I'm Dead (2024) is a controversial AI-generated comedy special that sparked ethical debate. Carlin's daughter Kelly Carlin strongly condemned the project, calling it 'disrespectful' and 'exploitative.' She argues that her father wouldn't have wanted his work manipulated in this way, raising concerns over the posthumous use of a comedian's persona without consent.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner relating to moral principles or the branch of knowledge dealing with what is morally right or wrong. From Greek ethikos 'moral, showing character'.
vocabulary.context: The AI-generated Carlin special received mixed reactions from fans and comedy professionals. Some praised the technology's capabilities and found the imitation 'entertaining,' while others remained critical of the ethical concerns regarding AI replication of a performer's persona without their consent, especially after death.

vocabulary.definition: A process or set of rules to be followed in calculations or problem-solving operations, especially by a computer. From Arabic al-Khwarizmi, the name of a 9th-century Persian mathematician.
vocabulary.context: The comedy special was created by a program called Dudesy, an AI that uses existing recordings of Carlin's voice and stand-up material to train an algorithm. This allows it to generate new 'Carlin-esque' jokes and monologues by imitating his voice, cadence, and attitude.

vocabulary.definition: An act of imitating a person for entertainment or fraud. From Latin impersonare 'to put on a mask', from in- 'into' + persona 'mask, character'.
vocabulary.context: Dudesy, the AI behind the special, explains: 'What you're about to hear is not George Carlin. It's my impersonation of George Carlin that I developed in the exact same way a human impressionist would.' The AI compares itself to Andy Kaufman impersonating Elvis, or Will Ferrell impersonating George W. Bush.

vocabulary.definition: The rhythmic flow of a sequence of sounds or words; the modulation or inflection of the voice. From Latin cadentia 'a falling', from cadere 'to fall'.
vocabulary.context: Dudesy explains how it created the AI Carlin: 'I listened to all of George Carlin's material and did my best to imitate his voice, cadence, and attitude, as well as the subject matter I think would have interested him today.' The AI studied Carlin's distinctive speech patterns and rhythm.

vocabulary.definition: Based on random choice or personal whim, rather than any reason or system. From Latin arbitrarius 'depending on the will of an arbiter'.
vocabulary.context: Carlin rants about God's methods of killing people: 'No matter where you are in the world, God can pull out one of these goodies from his bag of tricks to end your life at any time, for no reason other than he just gets off on making people suffer unnecessarily and die arbitrarily.' The point is that death comes randomly, without justice or reason.

vocabulary.definition: Tending to see the worst aspect of things or believe that the worst will happen. From Latin pessimus 'worst'.
vocabulary.context: After explaining how politicians profit from gun sales and won't change gun laws, Carlin acknowledges his bleak outlook: 'So, it doesn't matter at all that 75% of the country supports the idea of stricter gun laws. As long as the politicians keep getting rich, gun laws ain't going to change. Now, I know that sounds pessimistic, but don't worry. I've given a lot of thought to this subject, and I know the solution.'

vocabulary.definition: Officially permitted or approved; also can mean a penalty for disobeying a law or rule. From Latin sanctio 'decree, ordinance'.
vocabulary.context: Carlin explains how the gun industry works: 'Politicians have one goal: be rich enough to build their walls high enough to keep you out when the shit hits the fan. Make no mistake, guns ain't nothing but a federally sanctioned cash grab, plain and simple.' He describes the cycle of money flowing from buyers to gun manufacturers to politicians.

vocabulary.definition: Relating to or characteristic of a performance; done for show rather than from genuine feeling. From Latin performare 'to shape thoroughly'.
vocabulary.context: Carlin criticizes people posting tributes to celebrities they never knew: 'It's a touching personal tribute to a person you never f*cking knew. So please, stop it.' He suggests Instagram should let users set approved lists of people who can post about them after death: 'If you ain't on that list, you got to sit out of the performative grief competition this time.'

vocabulary.definition: Giving rise or likely to give rise to public disagreement. From Latin controversus 'turned against', from contro- 'against' + versus 'turned'.
vocabulary.context: Carlin builds up to a provocative statement about elections: 'I know everyone's getting excited for the next election. It's right around the corner, and I have some thoughts on it. Well, one thought, really, and most of you are going to be outraged by it. So I'm just going to say it: it does not matter who wins the election. I know that's controversial. The truth always is nowadays.'

vocabulary.definition: Fair and impartial; dealing fairly and equally with all concerned. From Latin aequus 'equal' + habere 'to have'.

The president makes too much money from the system as it is, and even if a president wanted to change it, they can't.'

vocabulary.definition: Recognize a distinction; differentiate. Also: treat a person or particular group of people differently in an unfair way. From Latin discriminare 'to divide, separate'.
vocabulary.context: Carlin describes the class of people who are 'completely f*cked' in America: 'You will have no access to quality healthcare and could die from an infected paper cut. You are at the mercy of an American legal system designed to discriminate against you, and you could wind up serving a life sentence for a traffic ticket.'

vocabulary.definition: Concerned with or involving the theory of a subject; abstract rather than practical. From Greek theorein 'to look at, consider'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'It will move on to the hypothetical. Theoretical situations… that may or may not occur. The female needs to know how you might respond.'

vocabulary.definition: To fit in with the wishes or needs of; to provide lodging or space for. From Latin accommodare 'to make fit', from ad- 'to' + commodus 'fitting'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'How did you procreate and cohabitate, learning to accommodate so as not to aggravate?'

vocabulary.definition: To live together as a married couple or in an intimate relationship. From Latin co- 'together' + habitare 'to dwell'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'How'd you navigate that? How'd you acclimate? How did you procreate and cohabitate, learning to accommodate so as not to aggravate?'

vocabulary.definition: To adapt to a new climate, environment, or situation. From French acclimater, from à 'to' + climat 'climate'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'How'd you navigate that? How'd you acclimate? How did you procreate and cohabitate?'

vocabulary.definition: To reproduce; beget offspring. From Latin procreare 'to beget', from pro- 'forth' + creare 'to create'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'How did you procreate and cohabitate, learning to accommodate so as not to aggravate?'

vocabulary.definition: The action of keeping away from or not doing something; evasion. From Old French eviter 'to avoid', from Latin evitare 'to shun'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'Avoidance is the male domestic instinct. Golf, the ultimate avoidance activity.'

vocabulary.definition: In a manner lacking sense or meaning; absurdly. From Latin non 'not' + sensus 'sense, meaning'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'A game so nonsensically difficult, so pointless, so irrational, so time-consuming, the world 'golf' could only possibly stand for: get out, leave family.'

vocabulary.definition: Not logical or reasonable; not governed by reason. From Latin in- 'not' + rationalis 'reasonable'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'A game so nonsensically difficult, so pointless, so irrational, so time-consuming.'

vocabulary.definition: No longer produced or used; out of date. From Latin obsoletus 'grown old, worn out'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'Talking is obsolete. It's antiquated. I feel like a blacksmith up here sometimes.'

vocabulary.definition: Old-fashioned or outdated. From Latin antiquatus 'made old', from antiquus 'ancient'.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'Talking is obsolete. It's antiquated. I feel like a blacksmith up here sometimes.'

vocabulary.definition: Operating at extremely high velocity. From Greek hyper- 'over, beyond' + speed.
vocabulary.context: Jerry: 'One of them operates on a digital, fiber-optic, hyper-speed network.'

vocabulary.definition: A statement that represents something as better or worse than it really is. From Latin exaggerare 'to heap up, amplify'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Built on provocation, exaggeration, and relentless inversion of social taboos.'

vocabulary.definition: A social or religious custom prohibiting or forbidding discussion of a particular practice. From Tongan tabu 'forbidden, sacred'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Built on provocation, exaggeration, and relentless inversion of social taboos.'

vocabulary.definition: The act of avoiding or escaping something, especially by cleverness or trickery. From Latin evadere 'to go out, escape'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Jokes escalate through taboo to expose what she sees as evasions, performative morality.'

vocabulary.definition: The outer limits or edge of an area or object. From Greek periphereia 'circumference'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Pushed to the periphery of society, we're still here.'

vocabulary.definition: The state or quality of being dedicated to a cause, activity, or relationship. From Latin committere 'to entrust, connect'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Some of you don't have the commitment it takes for a bathroom trash can.'

vocabulary.definition: Deprived of or lacking something, especially a non-material asset; sad and lonely. From Old English bereafian 'to deprive'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'If something were to happen to your dick, how would you feel?' 'Speechless. Bereft.'

vocabulary.definition: To express an idea or feeling fluently and coherently. From Latin articulatus 'divided into joints'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'It's a difficult concept to articulate. You know what I'm saying?'

vocabulary.definition: A reversal of position, order, form, or relationship. From Latin invertere 'to turn upside down'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Built on provocation, exaggeration, and relentless inversion of social taboos.'

vocabulary.definition: To move or swing back and forth at a regular speed; to waver between different states. From Latin oscillare 'to swing'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Her persona oscillates between aggressive bravado and self-exposure.'

vocabulary.definition: A bold manner or a show of boldness intended to impress or intimidate. From Spanish bravata 'boast'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Her persona oscillates between aggressive bravado and self-exposure.'

vocabulary.definition: Relating to the art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing. From Greek rhetorikos 'skilled in speaking'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Using shock not as an endpoint but as a rhetorical tool.'

vocabulary.definition: The quality or state of being ridiculous or wildly unreasonable. From Latin absurdus 'out of tune, discordant'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Crystallized in domestic absurdities and relationship math.'

vocabulary.definition: The practice of claiming to have moral standards or beliefs to which one's own behavior does not conform. From Greek hypokrisis 'acting on stage'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Moves through sex, gender, queerness, abortion, dating, parenting, class, and cultural hypocrisy.'

vocabulary.definition: A general agreement among a group. From Latin consentire 'to feel together, agree'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'The special rejects consensus comedy and therapeutic language.'

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of physical or mental unease; lack of comfort. From Old French desconforter 'to discourage'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Instead embracing discomfort as a way to expose contradictions, hypocrisies.'

vocabulary.definition: A combination of statements, ideas, or features that are opposed to one another. From Latin contradicere 'to speak against'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Embracing discomfort as a way to expose contradictions, hypocrisies, and unspoken assumptions.'

vocabulary.definition: A thing that is accepted as true or as certain to happen, without proof. From Latin assumere 'to take up, suppose'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Expose contradictions, hypocrisies, and unspoken assumptions in liberal, straight, and queer cultures.'

vocabulary.definition: To increase rapidly in intensity or scope. From Latin scala 'ladder'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Jokes escalate through taboo to expose what she sees as evasions.'

vocabulary.definition: To make clear and definite; to take a definite form. From Greek krystallos 'ice, crystal'.
vocabulary.context: Robby: 'Beneath the provocation runs a throughline about responsibility, crystallized in domestic absurdities.'
